candidate_problems.text: How can we distinguish institutions that persist because they still solve genuine coordination and legibility problems from institutions that persist mainly because embedded asymmetries make alternatives hard to see or adopt? This is the core falsifiability problem of the conjecture: without a criterion, every durable structure can be redescribed as both functional and domination-reproducing.

What mechanisms convert historically contingent design choices into apparently neutral standards of competence, merit, risk, and legitimacy, and under what conditions can those standards be redesigned without destroying the coordination capacity they currently provide? This is the main systems question because the conjecture claims domination lives in routines and definitions, but it remains open where the real leverage points are.

What would a non-dominating large-scale coordination system actually look like in an extreme case: can complex societies achieve legibility, role allocation, and stable expectations without reintroducing concentrated interpretive power in new forms? This probes whether the conjecture implies a genuine alternative or only a critique, and tests whether asymmetry is contingent or partly structural to coordination itself.
